speaker: interviewee
text: It's a life. It's so good.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's why it's perfect-- exactly what you're doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is roll two with Hellmuth Szprycer. Yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Szprycer. Roll two with Hellmuth Szprycer. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Huh? No, I'm saying, keep talking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister jumped from the van?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They stopped the van. The two gestapo got out. And suddenly, my sister, she was kind of a-- suddenly, one of the gestapo must have said, I'll come-- must have said I'd come, that-- that's-- and-- we go-- go back to our home again. He said, OK. Put the luggage. Go. [NON-ENGLISH]. Go. Yes. Yes. Until-- ba, da, da, then-- then-- then this is beginning January '43. It was only postponed.

speaker: interviewer
text: But maybe there were some Germans. Maybe he couldn't take it. He said, two old people? A little boy? A little girl, from 17? No, he said. And she jumped.

speaker: interviewee
text: She wanted to go back to her home-- run away. She wanted to run. Life, at that moment, it was already-- OK. We didn't know anything. But until March, '43, then it happened. Then they came again-- into the van, into the [NON-ENGLISH] deportation place. All Jewish people came there to be deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many. I can't tell you how many-- must have been at least-- oh, at least, 10,000, 20,000, 30,000 people. There were-- we were sleeping. And there-- there was school, [NON-ENGLISH] School. They emptied these schools. They were-- I remember we-- then already we had to sleep on the-- on the floor. My grandmother slept there, grandfather there, and I there, and my sister also. And when we were there a few days, then the war happened. Then one of-- they were all Jewish people who helped the deportation. They worked for the Germans. They had to do it. Some-- some schools, even directors from Jewish schools, gave away their addresses. It's known. They came up to my sister, and up to my grandmother, later on. And they said, your grandchild, Gisa, we have to take away straight away-- to send her straight away to Birkenau-- to Auschwitz. We didn't know where. They said-- I don't know if they said Auschwitz. She is able to work at 17-- a working girl. And she has to do-- separation. Oye! Oh, that. My grandmas and I got mad. No! Don't take my Gisa! Don't! And I, too-- [SOBBING] my sister, my beautiful girl. I didn't know, then, where. There was a fighting going on. I thought, I don't let that. But my sister said to me, Hellmuchin, she said, you stay with Opa and Oma. You don't have to go. Look after them. It's your duty now. Next day, she was gone-- deported-- to the train, somewhere. I don't know where. I was with my grandparents, and alone. I became, suddenly, very strong-- strong boy, of 13. Having-- suddenly, I became 20. I said, that's your duty now, to look after these two. We went, four or five days after, to Theresienstadt. We arrived in March-- got in on deportation-- end of the March, or something, in Theresienstadt, 40s-- '43. Arriving in Theresienstadt, in the ghetto, we went on ordinary train then, with seats, and every-- everybody was on it. We had luggage-- what you took with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: To a normal traveling, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, normal travelling. Yes. The furniture you have to leave behind. And the all-- the servant, all that, you couldn't take with you. The-- the-- the-- how do you call it? The [NON-ENGLISH], everything had to stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you sat, like mentioned, on the train, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like mention-- mentioned. It was a hard train. But, for me, it was a train, as a child. But I saw, already what-- where are we going? What-- what's happening here?

speaker: interviewer
text: Even in the kazerne, it's lying on the floors. But the days go. Do you know what? Everything goes so fast! You digest it without even thinking. So when we got to Theresienstadt, then it was already hell. It was hell already then. We got from the train. We had to walk quite a few kilometers-- my grandparents as well. Can you imagine? With the [NON-ENGLISH]? Or did you go--

speaker: interviewee
text: With the [NON-ENGLISH]! Definitely. Do you think-- and it was-- how do you call it-- springtime. So it's a good thing that was. But when we got into Theresienstadt, I was with my grandmother, together. And my grandfather got into-- the women and men didn't sleep together. He went to the other-- to a-- a-- a men's kazerne. And-- and that kazerne, it was somewhere. And my-- and my grandpas, he went. And grandmother and I, we went into what's it called-- into a-- a-- a-- a private house. There were private houses in Theresienstadt. And we-- they lived the first-- the few-- the first week, we lived on the attics. But I can remember, when I got there, on the attics-- no food-- nothing-- and fleas. I remember the first or second night, I got so bitten from fleas, and lice. I said, Oma I can't stand it. Because young people, young blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I can remember, she said, come. I take you down. And she took me down 3:00, 4 o'clock in the morning. In-- and downstairs there was a little fore-garden. There were-- Theresienstadt was a ghetto. There was-- it had-- had-- had houses. And there was a little-- like a little-- water comes out. How do you call it? You know, where you can-- A basin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from-- where you-- how do you call it in English? [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ya.

speaker: interviewer
text: A pump.

speaker: interviewee
text: A pump? Yeah, a pump.

speaker: interviewer
text: A well. A well.

speaker: interviewee
text: A well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and she sat with me, a stone on there, and I fell asleep until 7:00, 8 o'clock. Because there the fleas weren't, and so on. What I saw-- I looked around already. I start already organizing. My grandparents didn't get-- my grandmother-- they didn't get good food. Old people in Theresienstadt didn't good food, and very young people, also not. Old people died already in Theresienstadt, because they were not properly nourished. But I was running about again. I came into-- [BACKROUND NOISE] one day, it was about two or three weeks after, they called me from the youth organization, that I had to go into the Kinderheim. And that's how I knew. So I went in into the Kinderheim, like they say-- Kinderheim-- and school, and all that. There was a secret school-- Fredy Hirsch. This we had. [INAUDIBLE]. But, you know, I went there, and they were all Czech boys. And I couldn't stand. As I-- I-- I-- I-- I-- I couldn't understand a thing they-- they was-- they were saying. Later on, I-- I spoke Czechish, but at-- at that moment-- and I couldn't get along with these boys. It was-- I-- I had to be to my-- my grandparents. I-- I-- I-- there was something in me, you know. I said, no, no. I have to get it out of here and do something to help my grandparents. And in the Kinderheim, there, that they spoke Czech, and they sang Czech. Also, they all had their parents. All the families in ghetto had their parents. And they could rely on them-- their parents. They were in Kinderheim. And Tata came, and Mama came. But I didn't have my parents. I had my grandparents, which I had to look after. But at that moment, they looked after their children, still, and they were still safe, those children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did I do? I went out. [CRYING] And I went back to my grandma's house attics, and there was a cafe house there. And I went around to the square. And I heard music playing. And that was jazz music they were playing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was, in the door, at the top, it was a window. And I-- I climbed up, and listened in. And there was Fritzig Weiss, Ghetto Swingers. He was known as-- they called him the Benny Goodman from Theresienstadt-- other-- other players. And I whistled with them. They were playing 'A-Tisket, A-Tasket,' and I start whistling. And they didn't know where the whistling came from. And suddenly, he looked up. I say, come, ah! Fritzig Weiss said, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. Come in. I said, OK. [NON-ENGLISH]. And I whistled with them. [NON-ENGLISH SINGING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And he say, said, do you know what? We gonna use you in our-- what's it called? The orchestra. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had a big band. They played in every kazerne. I had to bring, always, these music boxes on the-- I pushed them. I had work.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the afternoon, it was Fritzig Weiss Quartet. And I was on the-- what's it called? On the-- on the stage? Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like you-- they had the-- there-- there was cafe house. People who were a bit-- because in Theresienstadt, there was a class. There was the Judenrat. And the Czech people had more advantage than the Germans. The few came from Berlin. We were outcast. And it was. There was different in Theresienstadt. But I said, not-- something not gonna happen to me. Because I won't hear about. So, what happened, I whistled with them, and I played with them. And so that, I got food. Every kazerne where they had a kitchen. I was called the [NON-ENGLISH] from Theresienstadt. So every kazerne I visited, and the-- the chef was always-- the-- the ones who cooked. They were old men. Ah, the [NON-ENGLISH] is down. And I got food. I got more than enough. And I helped my grandfather and my grandmother. And so in Theresienstadt, suddenly I didn't go back to the Kinderheim. I stayed in-- in-- in-- in a better place. My grandmother, later on, became-- so I was the [NON-ENGLISH] from Theresienstadt for a few months. Fritzig Weiss-- we played music from Lionel Hampton.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We played music from-- yes, yes, yes. And all these songs, when I hear in-- now it's [SINGING] They all played that jazz music. And the Germans didn't stop it. And we had three saxophones, clarinets, guitar, drummer, Viki Wahrfe, and we-- that was terrific. And I liked it. And that was my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these Czech boys, the group, didn't know me much. They knew that I was running about. I was-- how do you call it? Always on my own, fighting for existence-- not to get pushed back-- otherwise, I would have made-- my grandparents would have died in-- in-- what's it called? In Theresienstadt. It comes to the day, '43, December, that my grandparents are put on the deportation list, and not me. Because I was registered, always, in the Kinderheim, but I-- I didn't stay there much. I left. I stayed-- I stayed up in the cafe house. I was a bit of a-- what's it called? A black sheep, maybe, in one way, eh?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wasn't on the deportation list. I went to see my grandfather. I remember my grandfather, in those days, in the [NON-ENGLISH] kazerne. Every day, when I came to see him at 6 o'clock-- tefillins had [INAUDIBLE] with him. He was, at that moment, 75.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, Opa, I said, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Stay here, [NON-ENGLISH]. You're here with that children home. You play with the orchestra. Because my grandparents came also in the cafe house. They got cake. They got coffee. They were sitting, seeing their little grandson whistling with the band there. For them it were-- but-- and music is-- is a stimulant. It makes you happy. And made-- we made a lot of people happy in Theresienstadt, as well, when we played in these kazernes. Maybe one hour, two hours they were happy. And they were laughing. That's why that we were-- all that music existed-- classical music. They wrote songs there, [NON-ENGLISH] and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Coming back to my grandfather-- Opa, OK. My grandmother, you have to go with us. We have to find Gisa. And she was, oh. She was such a beautiful, good woman. But there, all her hair, not properly looked after; little dress, full of [NON-ENGLISH] --that position. I was always-- I was always fighting with God. I discussed it. Why? [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: The day came-- deportation-- on the-- after Theresienstadt, on the end, the barracks, there was the ramp with the rails. I went there. They were after the metal-- they were already on the transportation. They had all deportation number on them. And they were behind wires-- all the-- not electric wires. They were separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I stood there. My grandmother was on-- already on the bed-- not healthy. I said, what is here? She had-- you, know, she had [NON-ENGLISH]. She had two-- how do you call it? The upper-- upper hip. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that must have damaged-- been damaged again. And-- and I saw them standing there-- my Opa, my grandmother. And I shouted, Oma! Opa! I'm coming with you. I'm coming with you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't let you go. I said to myself, also. No. To the right, Obersturmfuhrer there. You see, the SS was always there. We never saw the SS in the camp, because it was Czech gendarmes. But now, I know, when it came to count each person, there must have been SS man. I don't know why. They wanted to count every Jew they wanted to kill. They have to see, personally, to check up-- it's right, or not right. A big man-- I went up to him, [NON-ENGLISH], which I saw in Germany with the Hitler Youth, and all that-- telling you I was brought up. My neighbors, they have [NON-ENGLISH] uniforms. And so I saw. Obersturmfuhrer, [NON-ENGLISH]. But he laughed, ironically, because he know where they were going. I didn't know. He said, ah, [NON-ENGLISH]. OK. [NON-ENGLISH], here's a girl. Your father stays here. And she cried-- a Czech girl. And he took her number and put it onto me. And I went into the wagon, onto the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, onto the cattle train. And I was happy-- not in the same as my grandparents. They were in different-- because they were already two, three trains-- I was happy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Suddenly, the door closed. Poof! Dark. You hear the train slowly driving away. Wintertime it was-- '43 December the 14th.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people in your car?

speaker: interviewee
text: Each car must have been about, at least 100-- 75 to 100.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened in the car.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hell. Hell happened in that car. On the way there, everything dark. We didn't have any food. Somebody gave me sugar from Czechoslovakia-- from Prague-- said, take sugar with you. Sugar is always-- you had that-- when you eat a little sugar, you have food.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the-- in the-- what's it called? In the cattle train, we were driving on that. To me it was that we would never get out, because people were dying in it. People did their commission in it. The-- they-- they-- they-- they had to-- water. They had to leave their-- you know, what I mean? The big commission? Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shit. They had to shit. There we were. By the-- by the time we got there, they were shouting, water! Water! [NON-ENGLISH] --the whole time, [NON-ENGLISH] , every meal. And suddenly, then, after three days, and three nights, the door opened up, you know. Raus! 4 o'clock in the morning, Birkenau, November. [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH]! At that moment, you thought-- we thought we were in hell. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In hell! You didn't know-- the lights, and the dark spots. They were standing on-- the SS-- with the dogs barking. They had pins in their-- in their-- in their-- hand sticks. You know, how did-- the SS were standing with pins in, because that was ice on that. It was like a skating rink. Raus! The people went out. The old people, they fell. [NON-ENGLISH]. The kapos were there. Mengele was there, [INAUDIBLE]. [NON-ENGLISH] was there, they're the icons. [NON-ENGLISH]! But these people-- these old people-- the three-- three days traveling. [NON-ENGLISH]! Some of them licked on the ice, and they couldn't take their-- what's it called-- away from it. They stuck on it. The SS came with a knife, and they cut the tongue. The blood was running down, and these people also fell dead. There was a cemetery, already, on that platform-- from 20, 30 people. In the trains, the ones who died were already-- hell. Everybody went to the right. I-- I want to see my grandparents. I didn't see them. On the train, when I went these three days, I said, oye. Maybe I'm going to see my Gisala. And it's going to be-- everything OK. We're going to be together-- imagination in this three day-- I've only got. Hell. That platform was not where the platform is now. Because that platform has been made later, right into the crematoriums. This was about a kilometer or two before the gate. They didn't have the-- the-- in '43, then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Raus! [NON-ENGLISH] What happened to the others? I went like mad. I'm-- I-- I-- I'll tell you what I-- at first thing I did-- when I did go on the platform, I shit in my pants. [NON-ENGLISH] I say. And I was-- I was finished. [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: We went into the quarantines. There were all barracks there. And people were, [NON-ENGLISH] are the people-- everybody shouting, water. Everybody was looking like that. Their eyes were glass, like, sometimes, in Mars afterwards for the-- the people you saw there. We were monsters! We had-- I don't know what it is. Without water, you can't exist. Without food, [INAUDIBLE], yeah, it-- it's possible. But the water-- so in the barracks, everything-- so, already, the kapos came in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Diamonds? You have diamonds? You have diamonds? You have jewelry? You have jewelry? I give you-- that was it. Do you have jewelry?

speaker: interviewee
text: A woman standing next to me with two children said, [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH]. I bring you directly a glass of water. I was standing next to her. She got the water. I said, [NON-ENGLISH]. Give me also some water. No. No, my two children. I-- it was already survival. It started already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Life for her family-- not me. So what did I do? I went out the barrack. And under the barracks was icicles. So what did I do? I start-- I stood underneath those icicles, and I got these ice drops, and oh. You can have the biggest bottle of champagne now. Ah, it felt so good. It felt so good.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't want to take off my pants, my [NON-ENGLISH] was so ice cold there. People-- so I just put them down. And I tore out my underpants. I tore out, with the shit, and I slung it away. But then the moment comes. I have to look for my-- my-- my grandfather and grandmother. I see my grandmother-- grandfather standing there-- from far away, without glasses, without anything. Because he was already-- he was lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, Opa! And he heard me. Hellmuth! He didn't-- he didn't think I was coming with him. He didn't-- he-- he said to me, Hellmuth, [NON-ENGLISH]? Hellmuth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got near to him. I put my arms around him. I pressed him against me. I said, Opa, I'm here. [SOBBING] He said, Hellmuth, mine Hellmuth. He was a man. He must have weighed 45 kilos. I-- that moment of strength, what I gave to him, was for me, the biggest satisfaction of my life as a child. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, Opa, stay here, I've got sugar. Sit here. I will look if I find Oma.

speaker: interviewee
text: I looked around. I couldn't find my grandmother. I came back. I couldn't find my grandfather. Because he didn't-- suddenly in the back, I saw him. The sugar was gone. There was already stealing going on. I got near to him again. I said, Opa, come here. And we go together. We still had our clothes on before the-- we had to go into the quarantine, into the shower. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took the clothes off. They said, [NON-ENGLISH]. Leave everything behind here! This is the same way you come out later. But we never came out the same way. We went out the other side. And he had-- but suddenly, my grandfather was standing next to me naked. I've never saw him naked in that-- my little life. He had two hernias coming out like this. I looked at him. I looked at him. I said-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --how is it possible? How is it possible to treat a man at his age-- family man, good man, a religious man, so thin, so small? In the moment, as a child, I felt a bit [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: To me, it was a bit, I don't know, it-- at the second I said, oh, this wasn't mine Opa. This wasn't that man standing next to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went with them through the showers. You see? That didn't-- gas didn't come out, otherwise I wouldn't have been here. Water came out. We went through. Then the-- what did they do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] They pressed the numbers into him, into his arm. They pressed the number into my left arm. This whole side there's not-- anywhere in the world. This whole side is here, Birkenau. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: But at that moment, when you get that pressed into you, your dignity-- you know what is-- this was-- this? Like a animal? What am-- you felt so undignified. You felt so unpractical.

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- you-- in that moment, you felt-- I felt I were-- I was nobody anymore. Because he said to us afterwards, my God, now this is-- when they ask your name, this is your number. Oh. With my grandfather, [NON-ENGLISH] we got our [NON-ENGLISH] clothes. We went into the [NON-ENGLISH] camp. He walked also with me, [NON-ENGLISH] camp next to me. I said, it's a good thing I was by him, otherwise he could have never made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before we got into the [NON-ENGLISH], before we got in there, somebody from the kapos there, give me a hit. [NON-ENGLISH]! Go in into the hole, eh? Into the-- into the-- march! Well, I said, this is different than Theresienstadt here. What's gonna happen here? So we went into the barracks. My grandfather was sleeping in the-- in the right, number 10-- who knows what it was. I can't remember that. And he was finished. He didn't have anything-- properly dressed. He was lying there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to myself, the next day, I said, now this is here something. This is-- I start thinking again-- organization-- what I did in Theresienstadt. I walked about the camp. I spoke perfect German.

speaker: interviewer
text: I found the first SS man. I said, is there a band here? Because I hear-- in German, I said, [GERMAN]. They come from Theresienstadt? Yeah. I went in there, and there were 1/2 of the Ghetto Swingers playing in the park.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was like a-- like a brother. And Viki Wahrfe was there, and thing. And he said, Hellmuth!

speaker: interviewer
text: And the SS man stood and-- and he said to me, you know them? Yes, he played and sang with us. He said, [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH]. And I whistled-- start whistling and singing to their jazz. And I said yes. [NON-ENGLISH]. You can start working here as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only there a few days. Soon I got already a job. But the first thing I thought about, to get some underwear for my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Continue?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I saw the-- it was a Vienna kapo there. And he was the kapo from the-- from the clothes barrack. For-- he had all the-- he had [NON-ENGLISH] --clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to him, listen-- in German-- he knows that. My grandfather's here. And he needs some underwear. So he gave me the underwear-- wool underwear.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went straight away into his barrack. I said, Opa, we also got in-- in-- in-- in the first [NON-ENGLISH], the children got white bread to eat. I don't know why they gave us white bread there. I gave it also to my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I put them on-- the underwear. I said, come Opa. He was laying down there-- to give him the warmth. It was so cold-- 25 under 0, you know? And he was safe. And I left again and did my work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I think after a week, I came in to visit him again, after two days or something, after a day. And he was gone. And the man said to me. Oh. The whole night he shouted your name-- Hellmuth, Hellmuth, Hellmuth.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a good thing I didn't see his-- his-- his-- his, what's it called? His dead body. I was happy I didn't see it. But straight away, I-- the world was gone. I went up to my grandmother's. But I found my grandmother in the barracks, in the back. She used to be a nurse when she was young. At 69, she helped in the hospital-- people. She helped. You see? That's what-- the Yiddish are intelligent-- helped, directly helped.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I came there, by my-- suddenly she got ill again because of what she had with her-- I went into the barracks, and she was in the upper-- upper bed, looking out like this. I said, Oma. Her hair-- not washed, not proper yet-- nothing. I said, Oma [NON-ENGLISH], I said, Opa just died. I said, he just died. [NON-ENGLISH]. She said, I'm gonna tell you one thing. Find Gisa. Make that you come out here-- that you survive to tell the family what happened here. I said, Oma, you're still here. I just want to tell you that. Two days later, she was also dead. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Suddenly, I came out, and I stood alone-- a little boy from 13 and 1/2-- in that ice cold camp. I spoke to myself. I said, oh. And then I-- I looked up to the sky. I saw the stars. And I counted each star, one of my family. I said this is my Opa. This is mine Oma. This is my sister-- Lotti. And I said, but this, I don't know. Like, maybe like a boat-- it has to go on a voyage. You have to have these to find your way. And that-- suddenly-- but the next day, a Polish kapo who worked in the potatoes kitchen, he found out about. I told him. I said, look. I'm going to help you. I'm going to give you food. You can always come here-- give you potatoes, kohlrabi. And it were.
